critique.summary: The article presents 'Electronics for Kids, 2nd Edition,' a book by Øyvind Nydal Dahl designed to teach children and beginners about electronics through 21 hands-on projects. The book covers fundamental concepts of electricity, circuit building, and digital electronics, aiming to provide a comprehensive and engaging learning experience.
mainPoints: The book includes 21 projects such as building an intruder alarm, electromagnet, sunrise wake-up alarm, and LED reaction game.
It covers topics from basic electricity to digital electronics with clear explanations and full-color illustrations.
No prior experience is needed, and the book is suitable for ages 10 and up.
The author, Øyvind Nydal Dahl, has a background in electronics and computer science and experience in teaching and product development.
The second edition is completely rewritten with improved visuals and explanations.
The book has received positive reviews from various sources praising its educational value and engaging approach.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the book's content, author background, and educational approach are consistent with the publisher's official description and external reviews. The book is a recognized publication by No Starch Press, a reputable publisher known for educational technology books. The positive reviews cited are verifiable and come from credible sources in the STEM and educational community. There is no indication of misleading or false information in the article.
considerations: The article does not mention the cost or availability of the book, which could be relevant for potential buyers.
It does not provide any critical or negative reviews, which could offer a more balanced perspective.
The article assumes a certain level of interest and motivation in electronics, which may vary among readers.
No information is given about the required tools or materials for the projects, which could affect accessibility.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the book 'Electronics for Kids, 2nd Edition,' with users sharing resources, seeking further learning steps, and asking practical questions about availability and suitability. Some comments recommend alternative free resources, while others inquire about shipping and age recommendations.

comments.author: eth0up
comments.text:
I figure it's fair enough, since this is a great but not free book, to drop https://www.allaboutcircuits.com/ - a truly amazing, and in my opinion extraordinarily well written and organized free learning resource.

Specifically https://www.allaboutcircuits.com/textbook/
comments.analysis: This comment provides a valuable alternative resource that is free and well-regarded, adding constructive value to the discussion.

comments.author: WalterBright
comments.text:
In the 1960s, Kosmos made the best electronics sets available. If you went through the kits, you received a complete undergraduate course in electronics (less the calculus).

https://generalatomic.com/teil1/index.html
comments.analysis: This comment offers historical context and suggests the depth of learning possible with older electronics kits, which is relevant and informative.

comments.author: cyberrock
comments.text: Not a kid but what are the next steps after this book? I've been trying to find the steps of the ladder between "playing with muxes and clocks" and "designing a USB3 peripheral", but that has been a challenge in itself.
comments.analysis: This comment raises a thoughtful question about progression in electronics learning, highlighting a common challenge for learners.

comments.author: viburnum
comments.text: Is there a parts kit sold for this book? I couldn't find one online.
comments.analysis: A practical inquiry that addresses the usability of the book, showing interest in hands-on learning.

comments.author: oytis
comments.text: As a parent I would appreciate if it came with an age recommendation.
comments.analysis: This comment brings up an important consideration for the target audience, helping potential buyers make informed decisions.

comments.author: madduci
comments.text: does anybody know if it will be available for shipping outside US? Seems a cool book, from the chapter preview
comments.analysis: A relevant logistical question about availability that could affect international readers.

comments.author: ifh-hn
comments.text: I love no starch press books. Nothing else to add.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses positive sentiment but adds no substantive information or analysis.
